ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mame aji karaage or deep-fried baby horse mackerel. FOODON_03302051 (fried fish food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fish, deep-fried) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for mame aji (baby horse mackerel) karaage in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for mame aji karaage or deep-fried baby horse mackerel as a specific food entity.

main_ingredients: wheat flour or potato starch (karaage coating)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of mame aji karaage is approximately 150 g, comprising several whole deep-fried baby horse mackerel with a side of spicy umeboshi dill oil tartare sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, horse mackerel, raw — adapted to deep-fried preparation; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15036 and analogous karaage frying adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for mame aji karaage exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for horse mackerel (jack mackerel, raw) with adjustment for deep-frying in karaage style: fat and energy increased to reflect oil absorption during frying (approximately +5–8 g fat per 100 g versus raw), carbohydrate increased to reflect starch coating (~5–8 g per 100 g). Sodium adjusted upward to reflect seasoning. Values represent a reasonable estimate for a whole deep-fried small fish with light starch coating. The tartare sauce is served on the side and is not included in the per-100g values for the fish itself.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of deep-fried fish with starch coating — analogous estimate based on fried fish with wheat/starch batter entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mame aji karaage. GI is estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous deep-fried fish with light starch coating entries from the University of Sydney GI Database. The primary macronutrient is protein and fat; the starch coating contributes modest carbohydrate. Deep-fried fish with starch coatings typically fall in the GI 40–55 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 12.75) / 100 ≈ 5.7, rounded to 5.8 for a 150 g serving (8.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5 = 12.75 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning and frying process
hypertension.guidance: Mame aji karaage contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from seasoning and the karaage marinade; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content from starch coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of about 5.8 per serving, mame aji karaage poses a low glycemic burden and can be consumed in moderate portions by individuals with type 2 diabetes, though the accompanying tartare sauce should be assessed separately for added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from horse mackerel offset by deep-frying oil absorption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Horse mackerel is a source of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which are cardioprotective; however, deep-frying increases total fat and caloric density, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and prefer preparations with less added oil when possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mame Aji Karaage (豆鯵唐揚げ) is a Japanese deep-fried dish featuring whole baby horse mackerel (Trachurus japonicus), a small pelagic fish prized in Japanese cuisine for its delicate, slightly oily flesh and mild umami flavour. The fish are lightly coated in seasoned wheat flour or potato starch and deep-fried whole until the skin and bones become crispy and fully edible, a hallmark of the karaage technique applied to small fish. The result is a crunchy exterior with moist, flavourful flesh inside. In this restaurant preparation at The Oyster Bank (Funan, Singapore), the dish is served with a spicy umeboshi dill oil tartare sauce on the side — a fusion accompaniment combining Japanese pickled plum (umeboshi) with Western-style tartare sauce and fresh dill, adding a tangy, herbaceous, and mildly spicy contrast to the rich fried fish. Nutritionally, mame aji karaage provides high-quality marine protein (~18.5 g per 100 g), beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and phosphorus. The deep-frying process increases fat and caloric density compared to raw fish. Sodium content is moderate to high due to seasoning. The glycemic index is low (~45), making it suitable for most dietary patterns in moderate portions. Clinically, the omega-3 content supports cardiovascular health, while the sodium level warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is not suitable for those with fish or gluten allergies.

meal_context: Japanese omakase or a la carte menu item

karaage: Japanese fried chicken style applied to fish

confidence_reason: No direct entry for mame aji karaage exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for horse mackerel with adjustments for deep-frying and starch coating. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy horse mackerel (raw) data with upward adjustments for fat absorption during deep-frying (~+5–8 g fat/100 g) and starch coating carbohydrate (~5–8 g/100 g). These are reasonable estimates but should be replaced with laboratory analysis or a direct approved-source match if available. 2. The tartare sauce (spicy umeboshi dill oil) is served on the side and is not included in the per-100g nutrient values for the fish itself; its contribution to the full meal would increase sodium, fat, and caloric intake. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried fish with starch coating entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mame aji karaage was found. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy horse mackerel data and are not directly measured for this specific preparation; these values carry higher uncertainty. 5. Halal status is not confirmed for this restaurant preparation; the dish contains fish which is generally permissible but the frying oil and sauce ingredients have not been verified. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input ('Alcoholic Drink', 'Japanese Rice Bowl') appear to be field survey platform categorisation artefacts and do not reflect the nature of this dish; they have been disregarded in this record.
